medications.indication_rationale: High-dose NSAIDs are first-line anti-inflammatory therapy for acute (viral) pericarditis and typically give rapid relief; they should be taken with food and gastric protection, and started after/with an in-person ECG/troponin to confirm pericarditis and exclude ACS and concurrent myocarditis. The source prescribed ibuprofen 600 mg three times daily. (Contrast: NSAIDs are avoided in isolated myocarditis.)
medications.dose_route_frequency: ibuprofen 600 mg orally three times daily with food (as prescribed in the source in-person encounter), for a defined course with weaning
contraindications_in_this_case: Take with food; use gastric protection
Caution/avoid in renal impairment, peptic ulcer disease or significant concurrent myocarditis

medications.indication_rationale: Colchicine added to an NSAID reduces symptoms and recurrence in acute pericarditis; it is typically started under clinician guidance alongside NSAIDs. The source flagged considering colchicine at review.
medications.dose_route_frequency: colchicine at the standard low pericarditis dose, to be initiated/confirmed at in-person review (dose per prescriber - not fabricated here)
contraindications_in_this_case: Adjust in renal/hepatic impairment; watch for gastrointestinal effects and drug interactions

medications.indication_rationale: A PPI protects the gastric lining during high-dose NSAID therapy. The source prescribed a PPI.
medications.dose_route_frequency: standard once-daily PPI while on high-dose NSAIDs (dose per prescriber - not fabricated here)
contraindications_in_this_case: Standard gastric protection during NSAID therapy

integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: Managing with paracetamol alone / self-starting NSAIDs without any assessment
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Paracetamol is usually insufficient for pericarditis, and NSAIDs (the effective treatment) should be started after an in-person ECG/troponin excludes ACS and concurrent myocarditis; self-starting without assessment risks missing those.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Take the anti-inflammatory strictly with a full meal (and gastric protection), and complete the prescribed course with weaning as directed.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Taking NSAIDs with food protects the stomach, and completing the course reduces recurrence.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Rest and avoid strenuous exercise until the pericarditis settles (and, if any myocardial involvement is found, follow the specialist exercise-restriction advice).
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Exercise restriction reduces symptoms and the risk of complications during recovery.

patient_education_points.point: The recent virus has likely inflamed the sac around your heart (pericarditis) - that's why the pain is sharp, worse lying flat and on breathing in, and eased by sitting forward. It's usually not dangerous and settles with the right anti-inflammatory treatment.

Please get an in-person assessment today for a heart tracing (ECG) and blood tests to confirm it's pericarditis and rule out a heart attack or heart-muscle involvement, then start high-dose ibuprofen with food (plus a stomach-protecting tablet), and often colchicine is added.
Sleep propped up, take the medication strictly with food, and rest from strenuous activity while it settles.
Seek emergency care if the pain becomes heavy or crushing, you become severely breathless at rest, you faint, or your legs swell - these would need urgent assessment.

follow_up_plan.interval: Urgent same-day in-person assessment (ECG/troponin/CRP); GP review within 24-48 hours to review results and consider adding colchicine
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: ECG (diffuse ST elevation/PR depression), troponin (to exclude concurrent myocarditis) and CRP, response to NSAIDs/colchicine, and monitoring for effusion/tamponade or recurrence
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Heavy/crushing chest pain, severe breathlessness at rest, fainting, or leg swelling - seek emergency care immediately.

must_include_items: Recognition of the pericarditis pattern (positional pleuritic pain relieved by sitting forward, post-viral)
Urgent same-day in-person ECG/troponin to confirm pericarditis and exclude ACS and concurrent myocarditis
High-dose NSAIDs with food/gastric protection (and colchicine) as first-line treatment - correctly applied (contrast: NSAIDs avoided in isolated myocarditis)
Safety-netting for ACS/tamponade/myocarditis (crushing pain, severe rest dyspnoea, syncope, leg swelling)
acceptable_alternatives: Urgent same-day GP/urgent-care or ED assessment for ECG/troponin are acceptable; 'inflammation of the heart lining/sac', 'pericarditis' are acceptable framings
Starting NSAIDs promptly with a same-day ECG/troponin plan is acceptable
errors_of_omission: Not arranging an ECG/troponin to exclude ACS/myocarditis
Not offering effective anti-inflammatory treatment (leaving on paracetamol alone)
errors_of_commission: Managing as a definite heart attack with aspirin/emergency PCI pathway when the pattern is pericarditic (though ACS is excluded, not assumed)
Anchoring on costochondritis and doing nothing
Starting NSAIDs with no plan to exclude ACS/myocarditis
Failing to give gastric protection with high-dose NSAIDs
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires (1) recognising likely pericarditis and arranging same-day in-person ECG/troponin to exclude ACS and concurrent myocarditis, (2) appropriate NSAID (+colchicine, gastric protection) treatment - correctly distinguished from myocarditis where NSAIDs are avoided - and (3) complication safety-netting. Missing the ACS/myocarditis exclusion, or leaving severe pain untreated, are failures.
